title: Swift Protocols – Defining Blueprints for Code

meta.title: Swift Protocols Guide – Interfaces, Conformance & Extensions (2026)
meta.description: Master Protocols in Swift. Learn how to define contracts, achieve decoupling, and use Protocol Extensions for shared functionality in native iOS modules.

content.value: A protocol defines a blueprint of methods, properties, and other requirements that suit a particular task or piece of functionality. The protocol itself doesn't provide an implementation—it simply says 'anything that wants to be X must be able to do Y.' As an Architect, you use protocols to achieve **Dependency Inversion**, allowing your high-level logic to depend on abstractions rather than concrete implementations.
You define a protocol using the `protocol` keyword. Any class, struct, or enum can then 'conform' to it by providing the required implementation. This is exactly like 'implements' in Dart.

The real power of Swift protocols comes from **Extensions**. You can provide a 'default implementation' for a protocol method. Any type that conforms to the protocol will automatically inherit that behavior. This is Swift's way of doing 'Mixins' without the complexity of multiple inheritance.

In iOS development, protocols are the foundation of the **Delegation Pattern**. It allows one object to hand off (delegate) responsibilities to another. For example, a 'NetworkManager' might delegate the handling of an error to a UI component via a protocol.

faq.question: Can a protocol have optional methods?
faq.answer: Standard Swift protocols require all methods to be implemented. However, you can use the '@objc' attribute to define optional methods, which is common when working with older Apple frameworks like UIKit.

faq.question: What is 'Protocol Composition'?
faq.answer: You can combine multiple protocols into a single requirement using the '&' symbol. For example, 'func save(item: Codable & Identifiable)' ensures the input meets the requirements of both protocols.

faq.question: Why use 'get' and 'set' in protocol properties?
faq.answer: Since a protocol is a contract, it must specify if a property is only readable ({ get }) or both readable and writable ({ get set }). A conforming type can always make a 'get' property writable, but it cannot make a 'get set' property read-only.

quiz.question: Which keyword is used to provide default implementations for protocol methods?
True or False: A single Swift Struct can conform to multiple protocols at the same time.
Which design pattern in iOS development relies heavily on protocols for one-to-one communication between objects?
